Data Overview
In this homework, we’ll be working with hospitalization data collected by the California Department of Public Health. Specifically, we’ll be looking at the number of COVID-19 and influenza hospitalizations in California during 2021. Download the covid19_hospitalizations.csv and influenza_hospitalizations.csv files from Canvas and save them in the data folder of your class directory
The first dataset, loaded as covid19_hospitalizations contains daily data on the number of hospital patients with COVID-19, by county. It contains the following columns: * county: a string, the name of a county * todays_date: a date, the day that counts were recorded for * hospitalized_covid_confirmed_patients/hospitalized_covid_suspected_patients: a numeric, the number of patients in the hospital (including the ICU) that have a lab-confirmed/suspected case of COVID-19 * icu_covid_confirmed_patients/icu_covid_suspected_patients: a numeric, the number of patients in the ICU that have a lab-confirmed/suspected case of COVID-19 * all_hospital_beds / icu_available_beds: a numeric, the total number of available hospital/ICU beds.
Run the cell below to load the COVID-19 hospitalization data.

The second dataset, loaded as flu_hospitalizations, contains daily data on the number of influenza hospitalizations across the whole state. title: Week7_statistical_models
text:
Disclaimer: Statistics Pre-Requisites
We assume that you have completed the pre-requisite EPI 258/259, or an equivalent introduction to statistics class. In this tutorial, we will teach you code needed to implement various statistical tests and models but will not cover the underlying theory of these tools. We do not expect you to remember which test to use when. Instead, we may tell you to run a certain test on certain variables, and you will be responsible for implementing the relevant code and interpreting the output. Getting you set up to run analyses in this way will prepare you for (often fast-paced) statistics courses and/or get you started on analyses with advising from someone with statistical training.
Unbiased statistical inference relies on proper study design, population sampling, and confounder control. For the sake of this course, we assume all assumptions are met, but checking assumptions is something you need to explicitly do in a research setting.
If we ever use statistical language that is unclear, please feel free to ask us to clarify.

title: Week7_statistical_models
text:
Categorical vs Categorical
Often when we’re doing research, we want to decide if two groups are different in some way. Last week, we asked if men were more likely to be smokers than women. Run the code below to reproduce a 2x2 table that shows the proportion of each sex that smoked.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
From this table, we see that 61% of males smoked and 41% of females smoked. It seems like males were more likely to be smokers than females. However, it is possible that in our randomly selected cohort, there just happen to be more male smokers and female non-smokers. R (and statistics as a whole) gives us a way to tell if that difference is statistically significant by comparing that difference to the likelihood of observing that difference by chance.
We can use a chi-squared test to see if the smoking rates by sex are significantly different. We use chisq.test with the sex and smoker variables like we used with table. (FYI, a chi-squared test is sometimes written as \(\chi^2\) which is the Greek letter chi squared)

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
From the output, we can see the test that was run: “Pearson’s Chi-squared test with Yates’ continuity correction”. The next line tells us the data we used, clean_fram_data$smoker vs clean_fram_data$sex. The last line tells us the results of our statistical test: chi-squared value of 164.11, 1 degree of freedom, and a p-value of < 2.2 * 10^-16. If you want to know what all of these mean, see this resource. We don’t expect you to understand what a chi-squared value or degree of freedom is for the class. For our purposes, our p-value is less than 0.05 which allows us to say there is a statistically significant difference in smoking rates between men and women in our sample.

Practice Problem 1
Do a chi-squared test to answer the question, “Is there a statistically significant difference in blood pressure medication use (BPMeds) between males and females?”

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Interpret the output here: …

title: Week7_statistical_models
text:
Categorical vs Continuous
Let’s say we now wanted to ask is there a significant difference between the ages of the males and females in this data set.
To answer this question, we will first use t.test(). A t-test needs us to specify how we want this analysis run in the format of a formula. If formulas ring a bell, we used them to tell table1 which variables to include in our table. A formula will look like outcome ~ exposure or dependent_variable ~ independent_variable. Essentially, the outcome you are interested in goes to the left of the ~ and everything you think affects the outcome goes on the right. For us, we’re interested in age ~ sex or the difference in age across the different values of sex. We then tell t.test what dataset we are using by saying data=clean_fram_data.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
We see a similar output with the data, a t-value, degrees of freedom (don’t worry about it), and a p-value listed at the top. The p-value is greater than 0.05, so the age difference between males and females is not statistically significant. Because we are now talking about continuous data rather than categorical, we get the sample mean of each group instead of an odds ratio. We can subtract these values to get that the mean age difference is \(49.79926-49.29341=0.50585\). We also get a confidence interval for that difference in means ranging from -0.015 to 1.027. Notice this range is mostly on the positive side, and our p-value was close to 0.05. Therefore, we can say that, in our sample, females are on average older than men, but that difference is not statistically significant.

title: Week7_statistical_models
text:
Linear Models
Linear models model…a line! They will have a continuous outcome like age, blood pressure, or a test score. You can then add independent variables that you believe influence that outcome. R will include them in the model and produce a coefficient for those independent variables. We will start with the most basic linear model with a continuous outcome \(y\) and one continuous predictor \(x\): \(y=mx+b\). As an example, let’s check whether age influences blood pressure. We will use the same formula format as we did above to tell R how to model the variables inside the function lm. Because these statistical models can do so much more than just test for differences, people typically save their models to a variable.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
We can then use summary on that variable to get the model parameters and confint to get the confidence intervals for those parameters.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The summary of a linear model has parts we are already familiar with. First, it will tell us what call produced this output so we know how the variables are being modeled. Then we get a big coefficients section that is the star of the show. Here we can fill in our values for our equation \(y=mx+b\). We can see the estimate for our intercept is 82.1 and the estimate for the age coefficient is 1.01. So our fitted formula is \(sysBP=1.01*age+82.1\). In other words, for each year increase in age, systolic blood pressure increases by 1.01 on average with a 95% confidence interval of 0.94-1.08. Each of these coefficients also has a p-value that tells us if that coefficient is statistically significantly different than zero. If the p-value for age was greater than 0.05, we could not say that the coefficient for age is significantly different from zero. In other words, age would not have a statistically significant impact on systolic blood pressure. However, in our case, age is statistically significant.
Some of you intrepid epidemiologists out there may be thinking that there may be missing parts to the story here. What happens if we have confounders, mediators, or other factors that we want to adjust for? With models, we can add them in using the + just like we would in an equation! We can also add in more complex variables to the equation. Often times age has a quadratic (squared) effect where its impact on health is high when someone is especially young or old, but not so much in the middle. We can add in quadratic or even higher order powers using I() inside the formula. This little function tells R to do that math before fitting the model. Let’s add in sex, smoking status, and age squared into our existing model.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
From this model, we can see that age and smoking status both significantly affect systolic blood pressure. For each year of age on average, systolic blood pressure increases by 1. For smokers on average, their systolic blood pressure is 2 lower than non-smokers on average. We will encourage you to take more epidemiology methods courses to learn what kinds of biases may be in place to make the model come out this way! The other coefficients age squared and sex are not significantly associated with systolic blood pressure.

Practice Problem 5
Do these same associations hold when using diastolic blood pressure as the outcome? Run a linear model to test answer this question and interpret your model output.

title: Week7_statistical_models
text:
Logistic Models
Rather than looking at a continuous outcome, sometimes it will make more sense to look at a binary outcome like the presence/absence of disease. For this case, our linear models will not do (because it violates the linearity assumption). Logistic models are specifically designed to model binary outcomes.
We use glm(family="binomial") to fit logistic models. Generalized linear models (GLMs) are versatile tools that allow us to model variables that have non-normal distributions. Binary variables follow a “binomial” distribution because there are only two possible values. By specifying family = "binomial", we’re telling R that we want to use GLMs to model a binary outcome. For more information on GLMs, please see this resource.
To run a GLM, we specify the formula and data, and can use summary to get the results in the same way as the linear model. Let’s model coronary heart disease (CHD) of diabetes and age.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The logistic model coefficients do not represent changes in the value of the outcome because there are only two possible values of the outcome! Instead, the model coefficients represent how covariates influence the likelihood of the outcome. In this output, the model coefficients represent the difference in log odds (as in \(ln(odds)\)) associated with a change in that exposure. For example, the coefficient associated with diabetesNone was about -0.91. Notice that R picked “None” as the group associated with the coefficient - this means that the log odds of CHD is 0.91 lower among those without diabetes.
However, log odds are a very unnatural and unintuitive way of thinking about disease risk. Luckily, there’s a algebraic trick that allows us to easily convert between differences in log odds and odds ratios. (See this explainer if you need convincing!). We can use the natural exponentiation (\(e^x\)) to convert model coefficients to their equivalent odd ratios. In the case of diabetes, \(e^{-0.91} = 0.40\) is the odds ratio associated with no diabetes vs diabetes.
To implement this conversion in R, we need use exp() on the model coefficients to get the corresponding odds ratios. We can access the coefficients using the $ on the model we saved.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
We can confirm that the odds ratio of CHD associated with not having diabetes is 0.40, or that not having diabetes was associated with a lower odds of having CHD. This association is statistically significant, since the p-value associated with the coefficient (as shown with summary(chd_smok_age_mod)) is less than 0.05.
From this output, we can also say that the odds ratio for CHD for each year older is 1.08 with a 95% confidence interval of 1.06-1.09. In other words, each additional year of age increases the odds of CHD by 8%.

Practice Problem 6

Interpret the output here: …

title: Week7_statistical_models
text:
Refactoring
In that last example, it is a bit confusing to talk about how not having diabetes decreases risk. We usually talk about things by saying that having something increases your risk. To get this set up correctly for our model, we need to change the factoring of the diabetes variable. You have been working with factor variables all along! Factors are how R deals with categorical variables, or variables that can only take on a certain number of variables. By looking at ?factor, we can see that R will let us change the order of our diabetes variable so that “None” is the reference group and we can get an odds ratio for the “Diabetes” group. Here’s how we change the order of a factor using dplyr.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Now we can see that having diabetes increases your risk of CHD by 91%.
Changing your categorical variables into factors has other benefits too. For example, it is much easier to see summary statistics about your variables. For example:

replace_na()

title: Week 5: Data Manipulation
text:
Removing duplicates
There might be extra copies of some rows in your dataset (can be a result of an upstream data processing error). If we leave them in our data, these data points will incorrectly carry more weight and introduce bias to our analysis.
We can identify duplicated rows in our dataset using the duplicated function, which takes in a data frame as an argument and returns a vector of logicals that are TRUE if a row appears multiple times in the dataset and FALSE if the row only appears once.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
It appears that there’s one row in the dataset that is duplicated; note that duplicated will not flag the first instance of duplicated rows. For example, if a row appears 3 times in the dataset duplicated will only return 2 TRUEs for that set. If a row appears 11 times in the dataset duplicated will only return 10 TRUEs for that set
We can view what rows are duplicated by indexing the data frame with framingham_duplicated_indices (see Week 2 for a refresher)

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Participant 46 had 2 rows in the dataset. We want to remove this duplicate, so only one copy of the row is included in our analysis dataset. The distinct() function can be applied to keep only unique rows in a dataset. The code below creates a new data frame framingham_deduplicated that removes the duplicated row we identified above, and compared the number of rows between the original and deduplicated datasets.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Week 5: Data Manipulation
text:
Handling (Erroneous) Outliers
Outliers are data points that deviate significantly other values in the dataset. These points can represent true, extreme cases but may also be a result of measurement error. In these cases, outliers can skew the results of an analysis, so it’s critical to account for any extreme values that arise from errors in measurement or data entry.
For example, BMIs that are very low can be fatal in adults and the lowest BMI recorded was 6.4 kg/m^2. In our dataset, it’s unlikely that we’ll observe extremely low BMIs (<5 kg/m^2), especially if other data points indicate that they’re healthy.
To determine if there are any outliers that need to be handled, we can first see if there are any instances where BMI is less than 5

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
It looks like 1 individual had a BMI of 3.4. Keeping this value in the dataset might bias our analyses. Instead, we can replace it with a null value since we’re not sure what the true BMI for this individual is. This can be done with the function replace.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
To check that the low BMI value was replaced, we can check that no rows in the dataset have a BMI less than 5. The cell below should return a data frame with 0 rows.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Week 5: Data Manipulation
text:
mutate()
Recall from Week 2 that we can use the [] and $ operators to add new columns to a data frame.
For example, the both commands add a column called age_months to framingham_df. These values convert the age in years (contained in the age column) to age in months.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
To add a new column using dplyr functions, we can use mutate(). mutate() takes any number of arguments in the form mutate(column_name = vector_of_values). For example, the following code could be used to create new age_months and age_days columns.

mutate can also be used to change the value of existing columns. Suppose we wanted to round the number of cigarettes per day to the nearest 10th and replace the values of cigsPerDay with their rounded equivalent.

if_else()
In some cases, we want to use an existing column to determine the values of a new column. if_else() can be used with mutate to set the values of a new variables based on a specific condition. It works similarly to if and else statements (see Week 2 for a refresher), and follows the structure if_else(condition, value_for_true_condition, value_for_false_condition).
Suppose we wanted to create a new sex column that contains the values male or female based on the values contained in the male column (when 1 = male, 0 = female). The following code makes this new variable using ifelse()

case_when()
ifelse() works well for creating binary variables but when we want to create a categorical variable with 3+ levels, case_when() is a more appropriate function.
Like ifelse(), case_when() reflects the logic that if, else if, and else follow. It takes any number of arguments that follow the structure condition ~ new_value (note how it uses a ~ instead of a =).
For example, the code below recodes education to more informative labels (0 = Unknown, 1 = Didn’t graduate high school; 2 = High school diploma, GRE; 3 = Some college, 4 = Bachelor’s degree or higher)

In calls of case_when(), conditions are evaluated in order; ex: the second condition is only assessed if the first condition evaluates to FALSE.

title: Week 5: Data Manipulation
text:
Examples
Below, we’ll walk through how each of these join functions merge data from framingham_df and zip_code_demog. framingham_df contains person-level data, while zip_code_demog contains zip-code level demographic data. The framingham_df dataset contains a column called zip_code that can be linked to values in the zip column of zip_code_demog. Therefore, our “keys” are the zip codes because they link the two datasets. If you want to join two datasets, you need to specify the “key” that describes how rows in one should be matched to rows in another. By joining these two datasets by zip code, we can merge individual health outcome data with information on the social determinants of health in their communities.
However, there might be zip codes that are in framingham_df that aren’t in zip_code_demog or vice versa. We can use the ! and %in% operators to find zip codes that are only present in one of the datasets

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Because the keys in the two datasets do not perfectly match, we need to tell R which rows should be kept in the join and which do not need to be kept. We tell R which rows we want to keep using left_join(), right_join(), full_join(), or inner_join(). In all examples below, we’ll treat framingham_df as our x or “left” dataset and zip_code_demog as our y or “right” dataset.

left_join()
left_join() will keep all rows in framingham_df, and only rows in zip_code_demog that match zip codes contained in framingham_df. Run the cell below to complete a left join on the datasets.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The new data frame has all of the columns from framingham_df, but now includes the total_population and median_hh_income columns from zip_code_demog.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Note how framingham_df and framingham_left_join have the same number of rows

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
However, we know that framingham_df included data for the zip code 01746 but not 01745. The resulting data set included all rows from framingham_df, so the patient who lives in 01746 will still have their individual data, but there will be missing values for total_population and median_hh_income.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Since no patients lived in 01745 and it was not in framingham_df, there will be no rows in framingham_left_join for that zip code.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


right_join()
right_join() will keep all rows in zip_code_demog and only rows in framingham_df that match zip codes contained in zip_code_demog. Run the cell below to complete a right join on the datasets.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Unlike the result from our left join, the resulting dataset framingham_right_join has more rows than the original zip_code_demog dataframe.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
This is because there are many rows in framingham_df that match each row in zip_code_demog, since there are multiple people that live in each zip code.
We can see this more clearly by focusing on how right_join works for a single zip code.
The cell below extracts the demographic information for the zip code 01701.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
We can use filter and nrow on framingham_df to find the number of participants that have the zip code 01701.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
When we right join framingham_df with demog_01701 by zip code, we end up only keep rows of framingham_df that have the zip code “01701”.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The resulting table has 602 rows, where each row contains an individual patient. However, each person has the same zip-code level demographics. We can confirm this by showing that there’s only one distinct combination of zip code, total population and household income.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Let’s go back to framingham_right_join, the result of the right join between framingham_df and zip_code_demog. The right join includes all rows from zip_code_demog, which contains data for the zip code 01745 where none of the patients live. Therefore, framingham_right_join will have data for 01745 but there will be missing values for the individual health factors.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
framingham_left_join won’t include data for zip code 01746 since it wasn’t in the original zip_code_demog data frame even though patients live there.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


full_join()
If we wanted all zip codes that appear in the data sets, we can use a full_join().

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Data for both 01746 (only in framingham_df) and 01745 (only in zip_code_demog) will be included in framingham_full_join.
The merged data will have missing zip code-level variables for 01746, and missing individual-level factors for 01745.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Analyses that use data created by a full join need to particularly careful of any missing values that are introduced in the merge process. In this case, if we wanted to use the number of rows in framingham_full_join to estimate the number of people per zip code, we might incorrectly count 1 person for the zip code 01745.


inner_join()
If we wanted only zip codes that appear in both of the data sets, we can use a inner_join().

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
In this case, neither 01745 nor 01746 will appear in the resulting data frame.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Analyses that use data created by a inner join need to be aware of which data points are dropped during the merge process. In some cases, the inner join could entirely remove patients with missing data leading to biased results.

title: Week 3: Functions and Packages
section: Function? I sure hope it does.
text:
Function? I sure hope it does.
Functions allow us to take an input, run some code, and send back an output. In fact, if you’ve ever taken a math class before, you’ve already seen functions!
\[
f(x) = 2x
\]
Look familiar? This function is very simple. It takes an input \(x\), multiplies it by two, and gives back the answer. You can run it over and over again for different values:
\[
f(1)=2*1=2\\
f(-10)=2*-10=-20\\
f(0)=2*0=0
\]
R lets us do the same exact thing. We can provide instructions inside a function that we use later for different inputs. R uses function(){} to define these inputs and instructions. The inputs go inside the () and the instructions go inside the {}. In this case, our input x goes inside the parentheses, and our instructions 2*x goes inside the curly brackets. We give the function the name times_two so we can reference it later. So if we want to turn our math function into an R function called times_two, we can say:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Run this code. In the Environment tab in the top right, a function called times_two appears. We can call to the function using its name followed by our input in parenthesis. For times_two, all we have to do is give x a value:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Each time we run the function, it returns back the last thing it calculated. For this example, it only calculates 2*x, so that is what it returns.
We can also use a return() statement to explicitly state what we want the function output to be. A return() statement tells R that we want to exit the function, and any code after it won’t be run.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Practice Problem
Write your own function called times_four_plus_one that takes one input x, multiplies it by four, and adds one. Then, call your function three times with different inputs to make sure it works right.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Week 3: Functions and Packages
text:
Built-in Functions
R has a bunch of built-in functions that help us get our work done. In fact, you have been using functions this whole time! data.frame() is a useful function that we learned about last week. And during week one, we learned about a bunch of math functions like exp(), log(), and even abs() which gives us the absolute value!
An especially useful built-in function is seq(). This function allows you to generate a sequence of numbers. Now, we already learned that the colon : can be used to generate numbers in order. seq() expands on this by taking three inputs: from=, to=, and by=. from tells it our starting number, to tells it our maximum number, and by tells it the gaps between the numbers. If we want the numbers 1 through 10, we can just say 1:10 or seq(from=1, to=10, by=1). However, if we want every odd number from 1 to 10, we cannot use the : and have to use seq(from=1, to=10, by=2).

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Practice Problem
Now you use seq() to generate all multiples of 0.2 from 0 to 5 (e.g. 0, 0.2, 0.4, … , 4.8, 5).

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Week 3: Functions and Packages
text:
Getting Help
R has a lot of functions built in, so it is almost impossible to remember all of them. Instead, we have the ? operator. When you write this operator before a function name in R, it will pop up some useful information.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
When we run this line, a little window pops up on the right side describing what sample() does. We learn that it takes a sample of size size from the elements of x. So let’s randomly pick 3 numbers between 1 and 10.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
There is also an optional input replace that is normally equal to FALSE. Further down under Arguments, we learn that replace tells it “should sampling be with replacement?”. In other words, should the same element be able to be picked multiple times? If we want that to be yes, we can tell the function that. Try running this code multiple times until you get a repeated number.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The help page on the right side also has examples down at the bottom to help us. You can also always use Google to help you figure out how to use these functions, but the ? operator works even without Internet!

Any Number of Arguments
Sometimes, functions will have an input that just says …. For example:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The ellipsis means that max() can accept any number of arguments and give you the maximum of them.

Try finding different minimums and maximums. What happens when you include an NA in the group of numbers? What can you do about that (hint: check ?max?

title: Week 3: Functions and Packages
text:
If-Else Statements
If-else statements give us a chance to decide what code should be run. They will look like this:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
We can use this statement to have code that is run in different situations. For example, if we want to tell the user if they try to do some math that doesn’t work, we could use an if-else statement.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
You can also use the if part on its own if there is not a second step you want to do. You can also put multiple if-else statements together if there are multiple decisions you need to make. Feel free to run this code multiple times with different x values.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Note than when a condition in an if-else series ends up being TRUE, all of the other elses are ignored. In the example above, x > 5 is TRUE, so x is 9 to 5 is printed. The everything after the else gets ignored. Even though the next if (x >2) would have also been TRUE, we don’t see it print that out because it is after the else.

Practice Problem
Complete the code below to write a series of conditional statements that compares the values of x and y. Try changing the values of x and y to check that your code works as expected

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Week 3: Functions and Packages
text:
Loops
Many times in research, we will need to run the same code over and over again, like if we need to calculate a value for every participant in a study. R gives us a way to do this using for loops. A typical for loop might look like this:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
In the for loop, we specify how many times the code needs to be run by giving it a collection of things, the vector numbers in this case. We give that element a name, number, and write code using that variable. In this way, the first time the loop runs, number will be set to 1 because that is the first element of numbers. The code inside the curly brackets runs. Then number is set to 2 because it is the second element of numbers. The loop continues all the way through 5 and then stops because 5 is the last element in the vector numbers. When writing your own for loop, you use the format for (variable in values). values needs to be a collection of something that you have already created. variable is a placeholder name that you can use inside the curly brackets of the for loop to do what you want to do.
Something we normally do inside for loops is add the result to a list of results so we can keep track of all of them. Saving your results is a lot better than just printing them out, especially when you have a lot of observations. Let’s say we have ten people in a study, and we want to categorize them by age. If we have their ages stored in ages, we can use a for loop to add their categorized age to age_categories. Notice that the part labeled “Step 2” is inside the for loop, so it will add new_age_category to the end of age_categories every time the loop is run.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: W1 - Install R & RStudio
text:
Welcome to CHPR/EPI 202!
We are excited you are joining us for the quarter, and we hope you are excited to get started with R. First thing’s first, we need to get R and RStudio installed on your computer.
Just a quick definition of terms:
R - R is a program that runs on your computer to process data, run analyses, make figures, and do much, much more. You can run R on its own, but it is all text-based. When something is text based, it is sometimes hard to stay organized.
RStudio - RStudio is a program that runs on top of R. It gives you a nice, pretty work environment. It makes it easy to see the code you are writing, how R is running, any images you have created, what variables you have stored, and much more! Working in R without RStudio is possible and happens in large data centers, but RStudio makes life much easier.

title: W1 - Install R & RStudio
section: Before we begin, what is this weird document format?
text:
Before we begin, what is this weird document format?
Can you believe I made this entire tutorial…in R!?!? Well…kinda. That’s more of a complicated story, but we will go into that during week 1. I am writing this tutorial in R Markdown which is a special file format that turns plain text into the fancy PDF that you are reading now. The extra special part is that I can include code in this document too:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Cool, right? Because R Markdown allows us to combine normal human-readable text and code, we will be using it for the pre-class tutorials and your homework. If you really want to work ahead, check out the Epidemiologist’s R Handbook on R Markdown after you finish this tutorial.

title: W1 - Install R & RStudio
text:
Step 1: Install R



R programming language logo



You will want to do all of this installing on a laptop that you can bring to class. That way, you can follow along with coding that we do together (and get a start on your homework!).
To install R, you will use the Comprehensive R Archive Network (CRAN). Click on that link or type cran.r-project.org into your browser.
At the top of this website, you will see a list of installation links.


If you are using Mac: Click the link that says Download R for macOS. You will see a page with download links that start with R- and end with .pkg. If you have a newer Mac that uses Apple’s processors (M1, M2, etc.), you will use the first link under the text For Apple silicon. If you have an older Mac that uses an Intel processor, you will use the link under For older Intel Macs. If you’re not sure which one you have, here’s a nifty tutorial on how to find that information on your computer.
If you are using Windows: Click the link that says Download R for Windows. Then, click on base. Then, click on Download R for Windows.
If you are using Linux: Click the link that says Download R for Linux. Then, select the distro you are using from the list. These links will bring you to a distro-specific tutorial for the commands need to download R.


After you have downloaded the correct file for your system, click on it. If you cannot find the file, check your “Downloads” folder or try step c again. It may take a little while for the file to download and install. After you click on it, you may need to say “Yes” to allow the file to be installed on your computer. Click through the installer using the default options. Eventually, the installer will say that R was successfully installed, and you should see R on your computer.

title: W1 - Install R & RStudio
text:
Step 2: Install RStudio



RStudio logo



To install RStudio, you can click on this link or go to posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop in your browser.
Since we already installed R in the above step, you can use the button under 2: Install RStudio to get RStudio for your computer. The website should automatically detect what system you’re using and give you the right one, but all of the different installers are available on the page if you scroll down.
When it’s done downloading, click on the installer and follow the prompts. You may have to click “Yes” to allow it to run on your computer. Keep following the prompts using the default options until you finish the installation. Then, open RStudio by clicking on it.

title: W1 - Install R & RStudio
section: Step 3: Check your work & take an RStudio Tour
text:
Step 3: Check your work & take an RStudio Tour

Click on RStudio to open it. It should look something like this:




Homepage for RStudio with no project open


If your RStudio does not look similar when you open it up, revisit steps 1 & 2 to see if something went wrong in the install. If you’re having trouble, please feel free to use office hours, email, or the internet to get help with your installation.

Let’s look around RStudio. The big section on the left hand side is the R console. When you downloaded R in step 1, that is what you downloaded! It has all of the power of R, and everything else on your screen is just trying to make your life easier. The > means that R is ready to accept a command. Feel free to click in there and ask it some math problems like 2+2 or 5*4 just to make sure it is really working. You can see at the top of that big pane are the tabs “Console”, “Terminal”, and “Background Jobs”. We will only be using the “Console” in this class, but it is good to know that there are other things there!




RStudio console with basic math problems giving correct results like 2+2=4


Note: Depending on when you read this, the top line may not say R version 4.3.2 (2023-10-31 ucrt) -- "Eye Holes", especially if you’re reading this in the way future!!! The top line will always be the R version you are running. As long as it says R version and then some numbers, you are good to go.

The top right pane is our “Environment”. This pane tells us about what we are working on in this project. When you save data sets or create variables, they will appear here in case you forget their names or what they contain. You can see there are other tabs in this pane too, but we will not use them in this course.




RStudio environment tab saying “Environment is empty”

title: Week 10 - Research Best Practices
text:
This is a new line.
For our last week of this course, we want to take a step back and take a look at some other tools you can use to make your research more collaborative and more reproducible. This material will not be on the final. Instead, we hope you absorb this week’s material to use in your future work.

title: Week 10 - Research Best Practices
text:
Why use GitHub
Collaborative coding is a little different than collaborating on, say, a Google Doc. When you are writing a word document, multiple people can log in and work on the same document at the same time. This type of collaboration does not work for coding. Imagine you are working on one part of the code that adds two variables together, but your collaborator is simultaneously working on another part of the document changing the names of those variables. Your code would refuse to run, and you would have no idea why! Rather than everyone working on the same copy of the code, GitHub allows everyone to have their own local copy, and GitHub stores a remote copy too. We call these collaborative projects repositories or, simply, repos.

title: Week 10 - Research Best Practices
section: Sign up for GitHub and install GitHub Desktop
text:
Sign up for GitHub and install GitHub Desktop

Go to https://github.com/ and sign up for a GitHub account. You can choose to use your stanford.edu email address, but remember that you will lose access to this account if you stop being affiliated with Stanford.
Dowload GitHub Desktop. GitHub Desktop installs all the programs you need to interact with GitHub and makes it easy to sign in, upload code, and pull code down.
After it finishes installing, GitHub Desktop should open, and you can sign in to your GitHub account.

title: Week 10 - Research Best Practices
text:
Create a new repo
Let’s put all of our completed tutorials for this class on GitHub so that we can reference them in the future. Remember that files you put on a public repo are public for the whole internet to see, so we don’t want to upload private files.

Go to github.com and click on the + button in the top right, then click “New repository”.
Give your repo an informative name like epi202-tutorials. Your repository name cannot have spaces in it.
Write a short description for the repo.
Leave the repo as “Public”. You should never upload patient data to GitHub, even if you set the repo to “Private”.
Check the box next to “Add a README file”
Under Add .gitignore, find the “R” option
You can leave License as “None”

So your “New repository” window should look something like this:

Then click “Create repository” You now have a repo epi202-tutorials associated with your GitHub account. GitHub will show you a page with information on how to setup your repo. Make sure to keep this page open because you will use the blue box for the next step!

title: Week 10 - Research Best Practices
text:
Add your repo to RStudio
Now that we made our repo, we can open that repo in RStudio to start adding and editing our files. If you have trouble with the tutorial below, try completely closing out of RStudio and opening it back up so that it can recognize you have installed GitHub.

In RStudio, click File > New Project… > Version Control > Git
We will then take the URL from the blue box on GitHub and paste it in the “Repository URL” field. After pasting the URL in RStudio, it should autofill the repo name. You should pick a good folder for this project to go in, like your EPI 202 class folder.


When you click “Create Project”, RStudio will open a new project based on what is inside your GitHub repo. You now have an epi202-tutorials folder on your computer that is associated with your epi202-tutorials repo on GitHub!
Take the completed tutorials from your class folder and copy them into the epi202-tutorials folder on your computer. You should see the files appear in your “Files” pane in RStudio.

title: Week 10 - Research Best Practices
text:
Push your changes to GitHub
We made these changes to our local copy. To send these changes to the remote copy, we have to commit these changes. You may have noticed the “Git” tab has appeared in the top right of your RStudio. This tab will help us interract with GitHub.

Check all the boxes next to the files. We are saying that we want to send those files to the remote copy on GitHub.

Click on the green check (circled in red) to review your changes. You can see all of your changes by clicking on each of the files. Added lines are in green. Deleted lines (if we had any) would be shown in red.
Add a commit message describing your changes, click “Commit”, and then click “Push”.


If we go back to our repo on github.com, we can see our files are now there!

title: Week 10 - Research Best Practices
text:
GitHub Workflow
As you and others work in RStudio on your project, you need to be sure to pull in changes others have made and push out changes you have made.

Before you start working, click the blue down arrow in the Git tab of RStudio. This button will pull in any changes that others have made, or say “Already up to date” if no one else has made changes.
After your code is working the way you want it to, follow steps 1-3 in the section above to add, commit, and push your changes to GitHub so that others can pull them into their RStudio.

title: Week 10 - Research Best Practices
text:
Why use OSF
Outlining your project on OSF provides you a public record of your project planning, data, code, and findings. This record can be useful if reviewers ever call into question your methodology. Some researchers may take an observational dataset with many variables and run statistical tests until they get a significant result. Registering your analysis plan before you do your analysis prevents this kind of bad statistics often called p-hacking. It also provides a single location to upload data, if it can be made public, to comply with the new NIH data sharing rules. For students, writing out your project plan in this form gives you an opportunity to get feedback from mentors and fix potential issues before you start coding.

title: Week 10 - Research Best Practices
text:
Look at an Example Project
Check out this OSF project by Dr. Ben Arnold.

On the landing page, you can see a quick summary of the project, the components of the project, and the recent activity on the project. Every action in the project is logged so that it can be tracked through time to see how the protocol changes.
If you scroll through the “Files” to “Scripts” or just click on “Scripts” in the “Components” area, you can see it is linked to their github tracking the analysis for the project.
You can click on the “Registrations” tab to switch over to see the snapshot of the project the author took before doing the primary analysis, capturing the primary analysis plan.

Consider giving OSF a shot in your research flow to organize your plans, code, and data all in one place!

title: Week 6: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Center
The two most common ways to describe the center of the data are the mean and median. R gives us simple functions to find these values.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
It is good statistical practice to use mean when your data are normally distributed (bell shaped), like for age in this study. The median is used when the data are skewed, like for cigsPerDay in this study.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
If we want to tell people the “center” age of our data, we can see 49.58 is a good central marker. If we want to say what our “center” cigarettes per day is, 0 is a much better choice because it represents the “average person” in our data set who does not smoke at all.

title: Week 6: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Spread
The most common way to measure spread is through standard deviations calculated with sd(). This class is not a statistics class, so we will not go into what defines a standard deviation other than it describes the spread of the data. We can also use the inter-quartile range (IQR) to describe the spread of the data calculated using quantile(). The inter-quartile range is two numbers associated with the 25th and 75th percentile of the data (aka the 1/4 mark and the 3/4 mark). To get the IQR, we tell quantile we’re looking for the 25th and 75th percentile using probs=c(0.25,0.75). quantile returns our two values inside a vector. If we want to collapse that vector into a character with a dash between the numbers, we can use paste() with the argument collapse="-".

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Just like before, it is good practice to use standard deviations for normally distributed data like age and IQR for skewed data like cigsPerDay.

title: Week 6: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Risk Ratios
The risk ratio divides the risk (or probability) of the outcome of interest in the exposed group by the risk (or probability) of the outcome of interest in the unexposed group.
Suppose we had the following 2x2 table:




Outcome +
Outcome -




Exposed/Treated
a
b


Unexposed/Untreated
c
d



Then we can cacluclate the risk ratio as:
\[
RR = \frac{Risk\ of\ Outcome\ in\ Exposed}{Risk\ of\ Outcome\ in\ Unexposed} = \frac{a/(a+b)}{c/(c+d)}
\]
In this example, we will divide the risk of CHD in those with diabetes by the risk of CHD in those without diabetes. Let’s start with our 2x2 table of diabetes and CHD.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The risk of having CHD for someone with diabetes is the number of people with CHD & diabetes divided by the total number of people with diabetes, or 40/(40+69). You can also think of this number as the percent of people with diabetes that have CHD; or the probability of CHD given you have diabetes.
The risk of CHD for someone without diabetes is the number of people with CHD & no diabetes divided by the number of people with no diabetes, or 604/(604+3528). You can also think of this number as the percent of people without diabetes that have CHD; or the probability of CHD given no diabetes. If we divide these risks, we get the risk ratio.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
According to this result, people with diabetes are at 2.51 times higher risk of having CHD compared to people without diabetes.
To avoid having to manually type the values, we can use dplyr to extract the relevant risk estimates directly from the data frame. Recall that when we have a vector of “0”s and “1”s (or “FALSES” and “TRUES”), we can calculate the proportions of “1”s by taking the mean of the vector. If we filter the data frame by diabetes status, taking the mean of the TenYearCHD column will give us the corresponding risk of CHD.

title: Week 6: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Odds Ratios
Sometimes, we want to know the odds ratio. Odds is very similar to risk with a slight twist. Risk is the number of “yes”s divided by the total number in that group. Odds is the number of “yes”s divided by the number of “no”s. If your risk is 50%, your odds are 1 to 1. If your risk is 75%, your odds are 3 to 1. If your risk is 10%, your odds are 1 to 9. Applying this concept, the odds ratio divides the odds of disease in the exposed group by the odds of the disease in the unexposed group.
Suppose we had the following 2x2 table:

Then the odds ratio is calculated as:



Outcome +
Outcome -




Exposed/Treated
a
b


Unexposed/Untreated
c
d



\[
OR = \frac{Odds\ of\ Outcome\ in\ Exposed}{Odds\ of\ Outcome\ in\ Unexposed} = \frac{a/b}{c/d}
\]
Let’s start with a 2x2 table of diabetes and CHD again:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
For our example, the odds of CHD for people with diabetes is the number of people with CHD & diabetes divided by the number of people with no CHD & diabetes, or 40/69. The odds of CHD for people without diabetes is the number of people with CHD & no diabetes divided by the number of people with no CHD & no diabetes, or 604/3528. If we divide these odds, we get the odds ratio.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
According to this result, people with diabetes are at 3.39 times higher odds of having CHD compared to people without diabetes.

title: Week 6: Descriptive Statistics
text:
table1 Package
We can have R make a table1 for us using the table1 package. I often use this to get quick summary statistics about all of the variables and then copy all of that information into a pretty looking table in a word document. The creators of table1 have already made a tutorial if you want to dive in deeper.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
To tell R which variables we want in our table, we have to give table1 a “formula”. Formulas in R use the ~ to describe the relationship between variables. For table1, we use the formula to add up all the variables we want in the table. If we want to remake the table above, we write the three variables that we want on the left side added together to the right of the ~ like ~age+smoker+educ_labeled. If we want table1 to calculate counts and percents for categorical variables, we use factor() to indicate that data is categorical like this ~age+factor(smoker)+factor(educ_labeled). If we want there to be different columns for different sexes, we need to add a | sex at the end. Lastly, we tell the function which dataset we are working on with data=clean_fram_data.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

You will need to filter out missing data for blood pressure medication before you give the data to table1.

title: Week 8 + 9: Data Visualizations
text:
Continuous Variables

Histograms
Histogram displaying the frequency of different values in a continuous distribution within specified intervals, and can be produced with geom_histogram()

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Using this plot, we can see that most countries have relatively low maternal mortality with only a handful of countries breaking 800 deaths per 100,000 births. We would consider this variable to be right skewed.
By default, histograms are generated with 30 total bins/intervals. To adjust the number or width of bins, we can set the value of binwidth. Smaller values of binwidth create smaller intervals (more bars) but larger values of binwidth create larger intervals (less bars).

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


Density Plots
Density plots also show the distribution of a variable, and can be seen as the “smoothed” version of a histogram. These can be generated in ggplot with geom_density

Density plots are especially nice because you can easily compare multiple lines on the same plot which is more difficult to do with traditional histograms.

title: Week 8 + 9: Data Visualizations
text:
Box Plots
Box plots can be used to compare the distribution of a continuous variable by different levels of a categorical variable. They are created using geom_boxplot. In the code below, we compare the distribution of maternal mortality by income group.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
We can flip the axes to view the box plots horizontally rather than vertically.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
When we do this, values placed in order from bottom to top. However, since we tend to read plots from top to bottom, we might want to reverse this order.

Adjusting displays with scale_ functions
We can use the scale_x_discrete, scale_x_continuous, scale_y_discrete, and scale_y_continuous functions of ggplot2 to adjust how axis values and labels are displayed in our plot. The function you should use depends on which scale you’re working with and the type of data it represents (discrete vs continuous).
For the purposes of this class we’ll focus on the breaks, labels, and limits options of these functions. Use ?scale_y_discrete to see other edits you can make.

breaks specifies where the “tick marks” should be placed on the axis. By default, the breaks will include all possible values of the discrete variable or evenly spaced points across the range of a continuous variable. We can change the placement of these marks by specifying a vector of values that contain our desired break placements (ex breaks = c(50, 100, 150, 200))
labels is complementary to breaks and specifies how we want to label the break points. This is particularly useful if we want to reformat axis labels without changing the underlying data. For example, if we had breaks that are coded as c("2012-03-01" "2012-06-01") we can create more informative labels by setting labels = c("March 3, 2012", "June 1, 2012").
limits allows us to control the range of values displayed on the axes, and the order that they’re shown in. For continuous variables, the limits can be used to specific the minimum and maximum value that should be represented on the axis. For categorical variables, the limits are specified through a vector that contains all possible values in the desired order. Functions can also be passed through to streamline the process of reordering variables. For example, rev reverses the order that values are displayed on the axis.

In the boxplot above, we’re interested in changing the order of income group, which is a discrete variable on the y-axis. Therefore, we can use scale_y_discrete(limits = ...) to change how income group is displayed on the plot.
One way to set these values is by passing through a vector with all possible values of the categorical variable, in the order that we want them displayed (from bottom [“least”] to top [“greatest”]):

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Since we’re interested in simply reversing the order of the variables, we can also use the rev function with the limits argument.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
With this plot, we can see a pretty clear relationship between income and maternal mortality. This relationship could be further investigated with statistical tests to determine if there is a statistically significant relationship between country income and country maternal mortality.

title: Week 8 + 9: Data Visualizations
text:
Stacking elements
A major strength of ggplot2 is its ability to add multiple elements to the same plot. For example, we might want to plot both the estimates and confidence intervals of mortality in 2020 for countries in South-East Asia.
We can start by plotting just the estimates of mortality using geom_point().

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
We can then add our confidence intervals on the same plot using geom_linerange(). Notice how geom_linerange() gets a separate set of aesthetic mappings (aes()) that indicate where the range starts (xmin) and ends (xmax). This adds to the first set of mappings specified in ggplot() (which also informs the country or “y-value” the ranges are associated with ) and uses the same dataframe.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Adding error bars is great practice for scientific figures because it allows you to clearly communicate and compare uncertainty in your estimates.
Similarly, we can add errors for each year to our line plots using geom_ribbon(). Again, we’ll pass in a new set of mappings to indicate where the error ribbons start and end. We can also specify the color of the ribbon by setting fill = "grey".

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Point shapes and line types
We can also vary the shape of points and type of lines in our plots using shape and linetype, respectively.
The possible values for linetype are “blank”, “solid”, “dashed”, “dotted”, “dotdash”, “longdash”, “twodash”. See this resource for a visual guide of what each linetype looks like.

The possible values for shape are numerics that correspond to specific shapes such as circles, squares, and stars. See this resource for a visual guide of what each shape looks like.

title: Week 8 + 9: Data Visualizations
text:
Varying elements by group
We often put data points from different groups on the same plot, but visually distinguishing between these groups could bring new insights to your data. For example, in our scatter plot comparing population and mortality, we might want to easily identify which points correspond to low vs high income countries. We can do this by adding another item to our aesthetic mappings (aes()) to specify that we want to color points on the figure by income_group.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
We can also change the title and position of the legend using the labs and theme elements.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Similarly, we can add a shape item to aes to specify that we want a different shape for unique income_group

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
In some cases, plotting and coloring groups on a plot can help improve how clearly it conveys the data.
Consider a line plot in which we want plot mortality over time for countries in South East Asia. When we run the code below, all of the points are plotted on the same line and we can’t distinguish between countries. This plot is uninterpretable.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Instead, we want to plot a separate line for each country. We can do this by adding a group item to our mappings to specify that there should be separate plot elements for each unique value in the grouped variable. In our case, this argument specifies that we want a different line for each Country on the plot.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
However, as is, we can’t tell what countries each line corresponds to. We can vary line color by country to be able to distinguish between them.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Week 8 + 9: Data Visualizations
text:
Facets
If there are too many elements on the plot, it can become too crowded to easily interpret. Instead, we can create many subplots and combine them with their axes lined up. This allows us to see individual data points more clearly while still being able to compare the different groups.
We can create subplots, or facets, by using facet_wrap() with the variable that we want to create subplots for.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
By default, all plots have the same axis scales. However, this can make it difficult to view subplots where data points that are heavily clustered. To allow axes to vary by facet, we can edit the scales argument to be equal to free (all facets have their own x- and y- axis), free_y (all facets have the same x-axis but their own y-axis), or free_x (all facets have their own x-axis but the same y-axis).

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Notice how every facet now has its own x- and y-axis. Allowing these scales to differ makes it easier to see differences within regions, but makes it more difficult to spot differences across regions.

title: Homework 5
text:
Data Overview
In this homework, we’ll be working indicators of noncommunicable diseases (NCD) like heart disease, cancer, and diabetes and associated risk factors collected by the WHO Global Health Observatory. Download the ncd_risk_factors.csv file from Canvas and save them in the data folder of your class directory
The dataset contains country-level annual estimates on the prevalence of different NCDs and risk factors that may contribute to these conditions. It contains the following columns:

country: a string, the name of a country
year: a numeric, the year that estimates were recorded
indicator_abbr: a character, the abbreviated code for the indicator
indicator_name: a character, the full name of the indicator
sex: a character, the total number of available hospital/ICU beds
estimate: a numeric, the point estimate of the corresponding indicator
ci_lb: a numeric, the lower bound of the confidence interval of the indicator esitmate
ci_ub: a numeric, the upper bound of the confidence interval of the indicator esitmate

Run the cell below to load the NCD data as ncd_data

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Run the cell below to see the mappings between each indicator_abbr and indicator_name. We can see that the data contains a variable NCDMORT3070 that describes the probability of death from a chronic conditions between the ages of 30-70, as well as factors related to blood pressure, hypertension, BMI, and substance use.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
